section.title: The Shoot from Jesse
section.sectionRationale: Immediately after the felled forest of Assyria's pride, a very different tree image appears: a tender shoot rising from the stump of Jesse, David's father, empowered by the Spirit to rule with perfect justice and to inaugurate a peace so complete that predator and prey lie down together. The chapter closes with the regathering of a scattered remnant, tying this messianic vision back to Israel's own restoration.
section.thesis: From the humbled line of David, apparently as dead as a felled tree, the Spirit-anointed shoot will rise to judge with perfect righteousness, secure a peace that undoes creation's violence, and become a banner drawing the nations and gathering God's scattered people home.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the Spirit-filled shoot from Jesse's stump and His righteous judgment; the peaceable kingdom where creation's violence is undone; the root of Jesse as a banner to the nations; and the second exodus gathering the remnant home.

units.label: The Spirit-filled shoot
units.note: wisdom, might, and the fear of the LORD

verses.text: Then a {{choter|shoot}} will spring up from the stump of Jesse, and a {{netzer|Branch}} from his roots will bear fruit.

The {{ruach2|Spirit}} of the LORD will rest on Him, the Spirit of wisdom and understanding, 
the Spirit of counsel and strength, 
the Spirit of knowledge and fear of the LORD.

And He will delight in the fear of the LORD. 
He will not judge by what His eyes see, and He will not decide by what His ears hear,

but with righteousness He will judge the poor, and with equity He will decide for the lowly of the earth. He will strike the earth with the rod of His mouth and slay the wicked with the breath of His lips.
Righteousness will be the belt around His hips, and faithfulness the sash around His waist.

units.label: The peaceable kingdom
units.note: the wolf and the lamb together

verses.text: The wolf will live with the lamb, and the leopard will lie down with the goat; the calf and young lion and fatling will be together, and a little child will lead them.

The cow will graze with the bear, their young will lie down together, 
and the lion will eat straw like the ox.
The infant will play by the cobra's den, and the toddler will reach into the viper's nest.
They will neither harm nor destroy on all My holy mountain, for the earth will be full of the knowledge of the LORD as the sea is full of water.

units.label: A banner to the nations
units.note: the Root of Jesse sought by the peoples

verses.text: On that day the Root of Jesse will stand as a banner for the peoples. The nations will seek Him, and His place of rest will be glorious.

On that day the Lord will extend His hand a second time to recover the remnant of His people from Assyria, from Egypt, from Pathros, from Cush, from Elam, from Shinar, from Hamath, and from the islands of the sea.
He will raise a banner for the nations and gather the exiles of Israel; He will collect the scattered of Judah from the four corners of the earth.
Then the jealousy of Ephraim will depart, and the adversaries of Judah will be cut off. Ephraim will no longer envy Judah, nor will Judah harass Ephraim.

They will swoop down on the slopes of the Philistines to the west; together they will plunder the sons of the east. They will lay their hands on Edom and Moab, and the Ammonites will be subject to them.

The LORD will devote to destruction the gulf of the Sea of Egypt; with a scorching wind He will sweep His hand over the Euphrates. He will split it into seven streams for men to cross with dry sandals.
There will be a highway for the remnant of His people who remain from Assyria, as there was for Israel when they came up from the land of Egypt.

choter.gloss: A shoot, twig, or young sprout, a fragile-sounding word for new growth springing from what looked like a dead stump. The image deliberately follows the felled forest of Assyria's pride in chapter 10, contrasting worldly imperial power with the humble, Spirit-empowered rise of David's promised heir.

netzer.gloss: Branch, sprout, a term closely related to the tsemach of Isaiah 4:2 and likely the wordplay behind Matthew's reference to Jesus as a Nazarene, Matthew 2:23, since Nazareth's name may derive from this same root, a small town aptly named for the humble Branch who grew up there.

ruach2.gloss: Spirit, wind, breath. The sevenfold description here, the Spirit of the LORD, wisdom, understanding, counsel, strength, knowledge, and the fear of the LORD, forms the traditional 'sevenfold Spirit' pattern later echoed in Revelation 5:6's seven Spirits of God before the Lamb's throne.

basic.context: Immediately after Assyria's proud forest is felled at the end of chapter 10, a very different image appears: a small shoot springing from the stump of Jesse, David's father, a picture of new life from what looked like a finished, dead line. This shoot is filled with the Spirit of the LORD, judges the poor with perfect righteousness, and inaugurates such complete peace that natural predators lie down with their former prey. The chapter closes with this same figure, now called the Root of Jesse, drawing the nations to Himself and gathering God's scattered people home in a second exodus.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a genuine messianic prophecy describing a coming individual ruler from David's line, whose Spirit-empowered wisdom, perfect justice, and peace-bringing reign go beyond what any historical Davidic king, including Hezekiah, ever achieved. The honest tension in the chapter is how the vivid peaceable-kingdom imagery, wolves with lambs, relates to the more clearly future-oriented promises of gathering exiles; the pattern of near expectation and ultimate fulfillment found throughout Isaiah suggests both a genuinely inaugurated present reality in Christ and a still-awaited fullness in the new creation.
basic.originalLanguages: A choter, tender shoot, rises from the stump of Jesse, and a netzer, branch, from his roots bears fruit, imagery so central it may lie behind Matthew's description of Jesus as a Nazarene. The sevenfold ruach, Spirit, of the LORD, wisdom, understanding, counsel, strength, knowledge, and the fear of the LORD, rests on this figure, forming a pattern later echoed in Revelation's seven Spirits before the Lamb's throne.
basic.typology: The shoot from Jesse's stump directly fulfills the promise of the Branch first introduced in Isaiah 4:2, and its rise from apparent devastation typifies resurrection life emerging from death itself. The peaceable kingdom where the wolf lies down with the lamb anticipates the full undoing of creation's fall under Christ's final reign, and the second exodus gathering exiles from every direction previews the ingathering of God's people from every nation into Christ.
basic.christ: Paul quotes Isaiah 11:10 directly in Romans 15:12, applying the Root of Jesse's role as a banner drawing the nations explicitly to Christ's mission to the Gentiles, and Jesus applies Isaiah's language of the Spirit resting on Him to Himself in Luke 4:18, quoting the closely related Isaiah 61:1. Revelation 5:5 identifies Jesus as the Root of David, directly picking up this chapter's title, the conquering Lion who is also the slain Lamb, worthy to open the scroll of history.
basic.god: God brings new, Spirit-empowered life out of what appears to be a completely dead situation, David's line reduced to a bare stump, showing His redemptive pattern of resurrection out of apparent finality. His justice for the poor and lowly, exercised not by mere appearance but by true discernment, reveals a character committed to genuine righteousness rather than superficial judgment, a character He now shares fully with His anointed King.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 4:2 first introduces the Branch of the LORD, developed further here as the shoot from Jesse's stump. Luke 4:18 has Jesus apply Spirit-anointed, justice-bringing language to Himself directly. Romans 15:12 quotes Isaiah 11:10 explicitly regarding Christ's mission to draw the Gentiles. Revelation 5:5 names Jesus the Root of David, the Lion of the tribe of Judah who is also the slain Lamb.
basic.oneStory: This chapter shows God's pattern of bringing overwhelming new life out of apparent devastation: a tender shoot from a felled and stumped line becomes the Spirit-filled King who judges rightly, secures true peace, and draws the nations to Himself. That shoot is Jesus, the Root and offspring of David, in whom the whole story of Israel's scattering and gathering finds its true, climactic fulfillment.

context.literary: The chapter divides into four clearly marked movements: the individual shoot and His Spirit-empowered character and justice (verses 1-5), the cosmic peace His reign inaugurates (verses 6-9), His role as a banner drawing the nations (verse 10), and the corporate regathering of scattered Israel in a deliberate echo of the exodus (verses 11-16). The transition from chapter 10's felled forest to chapter 11's tender shoot is a deliberate literary contrast between worldly imperial power and humble divine growth.
context.historical: Written against the backdrop of Davidic kingship apparently reduced to a mere stump, whether through the Assyrian crisis threatening Judah's survival or, read with a longer view, anticipating the eventual end of the Davidic monarchy at the Babylonian exile, this oracle promises that God's covenant commitment to David's line, 2 Samuel 7, would not fail even when the visible dynasty appeared finished.
context.canonical: Isaiah 11:1-10 stands alongside 7:14 and 9:6-7 as the third great messianic text in this section of Isaiah, and it becomes one of the most frequently cited Old Testament passages regarding the Messiah's identity, character, and mission in both Jewish and Christian tradition, echoed explicitly in Romans 15:12 and Revelation 5:5, 22:16.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To promise that from David's humbled and apparently finished line would rise a Spirit-empowered King who judges perfectly, secures a peace reversing creation's violence, and draws both the scattered remnant of Israel and the nations of the world to Himself.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is prophetic promise centered on a specific coming individual, not a general prescription for human political reform. The enduring truths are that true justice requires discerning beyond mere appearances, and that God's kingdom advances through humble, Spirit-empowered means rather than worldly force.

debates.question: Is the peaceable kingdom of verses 6-9 describing a future, still-unrealized new creation, a present spiritual reality already inaugurated in Christ, or both?

views.label: A future, literal new creation reality
views.case: The complete absence of predatory violence, wolf with lamb, lion eating straw, has clearly not yet occurred in the physical world, so many read this as awaiting the final new heavens and new earth, paralleling similar language in Isaiah 65:25.

views.label: An already-inaugurated spiritual reality in Christ's kingdom now
views.case: The reason given, verse 9, 'the earth will be full of the knowledge of the LORD,' is already advancing through the spread of the gospel, and the peace described may be read as a vivid symbol for reconciled hearts and communities under Christ's present, if partial, reign.

debates.bearing: Both readings can be held together without contradiction, following Isaiah's characteristic near-and-far prophetic pattern: the gospel is already producing real, present peace and reconciliation under Christ's reign, while the complete, literal undoing of all creation's violence still awaits His final, consummated kingdom.

study.originalLanguages: A choter, tender shoot, rises from the stump of Jesse, and a netzer, branch, from his roots bears fruit, imagery so central to Jesus's identity that it may underlie Matthew's description of Him as a Nazarene, Matthew 2:23. The sevenfold ruach, Spirit, resting on this figure, wisdom, understanding, counsel, strength, knowledge, and the fear of the LORD, forms a pattern later echoed in Revelation 5:6's seven Spirits of God before the Lamb's throne, tying this ancient prophecy directly to the risen Christ's own fullness of the Spirit.

typology.type: The shoot rising from the stump of Jesse, Isaiah 11:1
typology.fulfillment: Jesus Christ, the Root and offspring of David, Revelation 5:5, 22:16, rising to new life from what appeared to be a finished, dead royal line.

typology.type: The peaceable kingdom where predator and prey lie down together, Isaiah 11:6-9
typology.fulfillment: The full undoing of creation's fall under Christ's consummated reign, previewed now in reconciled hearts under the gospel and completed in the new creation.

typology.type: The Root of Jesse as a banner drawing the nations, Isaiah 11:10
typology.fulfillment: Christ's mission to the Gentiles, explicitly cited by Paul in Romans 15:12.

study.christ: Paul quotes Isaiah 11:10 directly in Romans 15:12, applying the Root of Jesse's role as a banner drawing the nations explicitly to Christ's mission of gathering Gentiles into God's people, and Revelation 5:5 and 22:16 both name the risen Jesus the Root of David, the conquering Lion who is also the slain Lamb, worthy alone to open the scroll of history. Jesus's own ministry embodies the sevenfold Spirit described here, wisdom, counsel, and the fear of the LORD, and His perfect, unerring justice for the poor and lowly, judging not by appearance but by truth, fulfills exactly what this shoot from Jesse was promised to do.
study.god: God brings overwhelming, Spirit-empowered new life out of what looks like total devastation, David's royal line reduced to a bare stump, revealing His redemptive pattern of resurrection out of apparent finality. His own commitment to true, discerning justice for the poor and lowly, rather than judgment based on mere appearance, is now fully embodied and enacted through His anointed King.

crossReferences.note: First introduces the Branch of the LORD, an image this chapter develops further as the shoot from Jesse's stump.
Jesus applies closely related Spirit-anointed, justice-bringing language from Isaiah directly to Himself in His inaugural sermon.
Paul quotes Isaiah 11:10 explicitly regarding Christ's mission to draw the Gentiles to Himself.
Names Jesus the Root of David, the Lion of the tribe of Judah who is also the slain Lamb worthy to open the scroll.

study.oneStory: This chapter reveals God's pattern of bringing overwhelming new life out of apparent devastation: a tender shoot from a felled and stumped royal line becomes the Spirit-filled King who judges rightly, secures true peace, and draws both scattered Israel and the nations of the world to Himself. That shoot is Jesus, the Root and offspring of David, in whom the whole story of exile and gathering, judgment and peace, finds its true and climactic fulfillment.

sermon.title: The Shoot from Jesse, the Nations, and Israel
sermon.why: Piper walks through all of Isaiah 11 in detail, tracing the shoot's character and reign, the peace of His global kingdom, the nations coming to Him, and the gathering of Israel's remnant, directly matching the four movements of this study and drawing out Christ's fulfillment of the prophecy.

quiz.q: What does the image of a shoot rising from 'the stump of Jesse' communicate?

options.text: New, Spirit-empowered life emerging from what appeared to be a finished, dead royal line.
options.note: This deliberately follows and contrasts with the felled forest of Assyria's pride in chapter 10, showing God's power in humble, unexpected growth.

options.text: That Jesse himself would be resurrected physically.
options.note: The shoot represents a coming descendant, not Jesse's own literal resurrection.

options.text: The permanent end of David's royal line with no future hope.
options.note: A shoot rising from a stump specifically signals continued, hopeful growth, not final ending.

options.text: A minor administrative appointment within David's existing court.
options.note: The imagery and the following description of Spirit-empowered, universal reign go far beyond a minor court appointment.

quiz.q: How does this coming King exercise justice, according to verses 3-4?

options.text: Not by mere outward appearance but with true righteousness, especially for the poor and lowly.
options.note: Verse 3 explicitly says He will not judge by what His eyes see or His ears hear alone.

options.text: By favoring the wealthy and powerful exclusively.
options.note: The text specifically highlights His righteous judgment for the poor and lowly, not the powerful.

options.text: By delegating all judgment to human courts.
options.note: The passage describes His own direct, personal exercise of judgment, not delegation.

options.text: By avoiding judgment altogether.
options.note: The text explicitly describes Him actively judging and deciding cases, not abstaining from judgment.

quiz.q: What does the peaceable kingdom imagery of verses 6-9, the wolf with the lamb, communicate?

options.text: A complete undoing of creation's violence, rooted in the earth being full of the knowledge of the LORD.
options.note: Verse 9 explicitly gives the reason: universal knowledge of the LORD produces this comprehensive peace.

options.text: A description of an ancient zoo exhibit.
options.note: This is symbolic, theological imagery about the effects of the Messiah's reign, not a literal zoological report.

options.text: A warning that predators will become more dangerous.
options.note: The imagery describes reconciliation and safety, the opposite of increased danger.

options.text: A purely political statement about international alliances.
options.note: The imagery is cosmic and creational in scope, rooted in universal knowledge of God, not merely political alliance.

quiz.q: How does Paul use Isaiah 11:10 in Romans 15:12?

options.text: He quotes it directly to show that the Root of Jesse's mission includes drawing the Gentiles, fulfilled in Christ.
options.note: This ties the passage's messianic hope directly to the New Testament church's mission to the nations.

options.text: He argues the verse applies only to ethnic Israel with no relevance to Gentiles.
options.note: Paul specifically applies the verse to Gentile inclusion in God's people.

options.text: He rejects the verse as inapplicable to the church age.
options.note: Paul affirms and applies the verse as fulfilled and relevant, not rejects it.

options.text: He uses it to argue against any Gentile mission.
options.note: Paul uses it to support, not oppose, the mission to the Gentiles.

quiz.q: What does the 'second exodus' language of verses 11-16 emphasize about the coming regathering?

options.text: God will gather His scattered people from every direction just as decisively as He once brought Israel out of Egypt.
options.note: The explicit comparison to the exodus from Egypt in verse 16 frames this as a comparably decisive act of divine deliverance.

options.text: It describes a minor, insignificant regional relocation.
options.note: The passage names numerous distant regions, Assyria, Egypt, Elam, Shinar, and the islands of the sea, indicating a comprehensive, significant gathering.

options.text: It predicts the exiles will never actually return.
options.note: The passage promises an actual, decisive gathering, not permanent exile.

options.text: It focuses exclusively on the northern kingdom, excluding Judah.
options.note: The text explicitly names both 'the exiles of Israel' and 'the scattered of Judah' together.

questions: This chapter's shoot rises from what looked like a completely dead stump. Where in your own life or circumstances do you need to trust that God can bring new life out of what feels finished or hopeless?

The coming King judges not by mere appearance but with true discernment for the poor and lowly. Where might you be tempted to judge people or situations only by surface impressions rather than seeking real understanding?

The peaceable kingdom flows from the earth being full of the knowledge of the LORD. How is growing in your own knowledge of God currently producing more peace, or less anxiety, in your relationships and circumstances?
The Root of Jesse becomes a banner drawing the nations to Himself. How are you actively participating in God's mission to draw people from every background to Christ?

God promised to gather His scattered people as decisively as He once delivered Israel from Egypt. What situation in your own life do you need to trust God to gather, restore, or redeem as thoroughly as that first great deliverance?

translationNotes.note: 'Branch,' netzer, is a different Hebrew word from the tsemach, Branch, of Isaiah 4:2, though both carry similar messianic freight; netzer may be reflected in Matthew's wordplay identifying Jesus as a Nazarene, Matthew 2:23.

'The rod of His mouth' and 'the breath of His lips' describe the power of His spoken word to execute judgment, imagery Paul draws on directly in 2 Thessalonians 2:8 describing the defeat of the lawless one at Christ's coming.

'A second time' explicitly frames this regathering as a second exodus, comparable to the first deliverance from Egypt, and the long list of regions named reflects the wide scattering of Israelites across the ancient Near East by this point in history.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from the Makor scripture toolkit for Isaiah 11:1-16.
